summary:
The rock art of Arnhem Land in the Northern Territory of Australia represents one of the world's most extensive and ancient traditions of visual expression, with some images in the region potentially dating back 28,000 years or more, though robust dating of most rock art remains challenging. By 10,000–8,000 BCE, rock art production in Arnhem Land was well established and reflects the rich ceremonial and cultural life of Aboriginal Australian communities inhabiting this tropical landscape.

Arnhem Land rock art documents significant environmental and cultural change: early pre-estuarine phases depict the fauna of a now-submerged coastal plain, including megafauna and animals associated with drier environments, while later phases reflect the flooding of coastal lowlands as sea levels rose with the [[event:1262|end of the Ice Age]] and the appearance of the estuarine and monsoon environment that characterizes the region today. The rock art of Arnhem Land includes detailed depictions of fish, turtles, crocodiles, and other species that colonized the region as coastal habitats changed, as well as human figures, spiritual beings, and elaborate X-ray style animal depictions that became one of the most recognizable artistic traditions of Aboriginal Australia. The artistic tradition of Arnhem Land has continuity extending into the present day, making it one of the world's oldest living artistic traditions.
